revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''諏訪 円忠'''（すわ えんちゅう、（[[永仁]]3年）[[1295年]] - （[[貞治]]3年）[[1364年]]）は[[鎌倉幕府]]と[[室町幕府]]の奉...'
revision.text:
'''諏訪 円忠'''（すわ えんちゅう、（[[永仁]]3年）[[1295年]] - （[[貞治]]3年）[[1364年]]）は[[鎌倉幕府]]と[[室町幕府]]の奉行人。
== 人物 == [[信濃国]][[諏訪郡]]生まれ。諏訪盛忠の子。[[諏訪神党]]の『神氏系図』によれば[[諏訪大社]]上社の支族で、[[埴科郡]]船山郷の[[地頭]]小坂氏を継いだので小坂円忠とも称する。諏訪氏は[[北条氏]]の得宗[[被官]]で、叔父の諏訪時光も鎌倉幕府の奉行人であった関係から、その養子となり鎌倉で元服した。
北条氏が滅亡すると上洛し、[[夢窓疎石]]の仲介により[[足利尊氏]]に仕官し、室町幕府の[[評定衆]]から守護奉行を経て、[[天竜寺]]の造営奉行を務めた。[[中先代の乱]]以後に衰亡した諏訪宗家の再興にも助力し、諏訪上社の花会が信濃一円の武士の御頭奉仕で行われるようになった。
[[延文]]元年（1356年）、尊氏の奥書を持つ『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』を完成させ、当時の公家社会をはじめとする各方面に諏訪信仰を普及させたほか、尊氏が発願した一国一寺の[[安国寺利生塔|安国寺]]創建にあたり、信濃安国寺を諏訪上社の近くに建て、夢窓疎石を開山者とした。
== 関連項目 == * [[諏訪氏]]
== 参考文献 == *『長野県歴史人物大事典』 郷土出版社、1989年 *『信州の人物余聞』ほおずき書籍、2010年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すわ えんちゆう}} [[Category:諏訪氏|えんちゆう]] [[Category:鎌倉時代の人物]] [[Category:室町時代の人物]] [[Category:1295年生]] [[Category:1364年没]]

'''諏訪 円忠'''（すわ えんちゅう、[[永仁]]3年（[[1295年]]） - [[貞治]]3年（[[1364年]]））は[[鎌倉幕府]]と[[室町幕府]]の奉行人。
== 人物 == [[信濃国]][[諏訪郡]]生まれ。諏訪盛忠の子。[[諏訪神党]]の『神氏系図』によれば[[諏訪大社]]上社の支族で、[[埴科郡]]船山郷の[[地頭]]小坂氏を継いだので小坂円忠とも称する。諏訪氏は[[北条氏]]の得宗[[被官]]で、叔父の諏訪時光も鎌倉幕府の奉行人であった関係から、その養子となり鎌倉で元服した。
北条氏が滅亡すると上洛し、[[夢窓疎石]]の仲介により[[足利尊氏]]に仕官し、室町幕府の[[評定衆]]から守護奉行を経て、[[天竜寺]]の造営奉行を務めた。[[中先代の乱]]以後に衰亡した諏訪宗家の再興にも助力し、諏訪上社の花会が信濃一円の武士の御頭奉仕で行われるようになった。
[[延文]]元年（1356年）、尊氏の奥書を持つ『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』を完成させ、当時の公家社会をはじめとする各方面に諏訪信仰を普及させたほか、尊氏が発願した一国一寺の[[安国寺利生塔|安国寺]]創建にあたり、信濃安国寺を諏訪上社の近くに建て、夢窓疎石を開山者とした。

== 参考文献 == *『長野県歴史人物大事典』 郷土出版社、1989年 *『信州の人物余聞』ほおずき書籍、2010年

'''諏訪 円忠'''（すわ えんちゅう、[[永仁]]3年（[[1295年]]） - [[貞治]]3年（[[1364年]]））は[[鎌倉幕府]]と[[室町幕府]]の奉行人。[[諏訪氏|京都諏訪氏]]の祖。
== 人物 == [[信濃国]][[諏訪郡]]生まれ。諏訪盛忠の子。[[諏訪神党]]の『神氏系図』によれば[[諏訪大社]]上社の支族で、[[埴科郡]]船山郷の[[地頭]]小坂氏を継いだので小坂円忠とも称する。諏訪氏は[[北条氏]]の得宗[[被官]]で、叔父の諏訪時光も鎌倉幕府の奉行人であった関係から、その養子となり鎌倉で元服した。
北条氏が滅亡すると上洛し、[[夢窓疎石]]の仲介により[[足利尊氏]]に仕官し、室町幕府の[[評定衆]]から守護奉行を経て、[[天竜寺]]の造営奉行を務めた。さらに[[僧録|禅律方]]奉行人となり主に[[禅宗]]寺院関連の案件を担当した。[[中先代の乱]]以後に衰亡した諏訪宗家の再興にも助力し、諏訪上社の花会が信濃一円の武士の御頭奉仕で行われるようになった。
[[延文]]元年（1356年）、尊氏の奥書を持つ『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』を完成させ、当時の公家社会をはじめとする各方面に諏訪信仰を普及させたほか、尊氏が発願した一国一寺の[[安国寺利生塔]]創建にあたり、[[安国寺 (茅野市)|信濃安国寺]]を諏訪上社の近くに建て、夢窓疎石を開山者とした。
また円忠は諏訪神党[[祢津氏]]が相伝した[[鷹匠]]の秘術の故事を受け継ぎ（『神氏系図』）、子孫の京都諏訪氏の惣領に代々伝授され、[[文明 (日本)|文明]]5年（1473年）には[[飛鳥井家|飛鳥井氏]]と諏訪忠郷が鷹に関する[[和歌]]に註釈をつけて[[将軍]][[足利義政]]の上覧に応えている（『鷹百首註』）。

== 人物 == [[信濃国]][[諏訪郡]]生まれ。諏訪盛忠の子。[[諏訪神党]]の『神氏系図』によれば[[諏訪大社]]上社の支族で、[[埴科郡]]船山郷の[[地頭]]小坂氏を継いだので小坂円忠とも称する。諏訪氏は[[北条氏]]の得宗[[被官]]で、叔父の諏訪時光も鎌倉幕府の奉行人であった関係から、その養子となり鎌倉で元服した。
北条氏が滅亡すると上洛し、[[夢窓疎石]]の仲介により[[足利尊氏]]に仕官し、室町幕府の[[評定衆]]から守護奉行を経て、[[天竜寺]]の造営奉行を務めた。さらに[[僧録|禅律方]]奉行人となり主に[[禅宗]]寺院関連の案件を担当した。[[中先代の乱]]以後に衰亡した諏訪宗家の再興にも助力し、諏訪上社の花会が信濃一円の武士の御頭奉仕で行われるようになった。
[[延文]]元年（1356年）、尊氏の奥書を持つ『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』を完成させ、当時の公家社会をはじめとする各方面に諏訪信仰を普及させたほか、尊氏が発願した一国一寺の[[安国寺利生塔]]創建にあたり、[[安国寺 (茅野市)|信濃安国寺]]を諏訪上社の近くに建て、夢窓疎石を開山者とした。
また円忠は諏訪神党[[祢津氏]]が相伝した[[鷹匠]]の秘術の[[故実]]を受け継ぎ（『神氏系図』）、子孫の京都諏訪氏の惣領に代々伝授され、[[文明 (日本)|文明]]5年（1473年）には[[飛鳥井家|飛鳥井氏]]と諏訪忠郷が鷹に関する[[和歌]]に註釈をつけて[[将軍]][[足利義政]]の上覧に応えている（『鷹百首註』）。忠郷の子諏訪貞道も『鷹聞書少々』という鷹狩の故実書を編纂している。
== 関連項目 == * [[諏訪氏]]
== 参考文献 == *『長野県歴史人物大事典』 [[郷土出版社]]、1989年 *『信州の人物余聞』[[ほおずき書籍]]、2010年

== 人物 == [[信濃国]][[諏訪郡]]生まれ。諏訪盛忠の子。[[諏訪神党]]の『神氏系図』によれば[[諏訪大社]]上社の支族で、[[埴科郡]]船山郷の[[地頭]]小坂氏を継いだので小坂円忠とも称する。諏訪氏は[[北条氏]]の得宗[[被官]]で、叔父の諏訪時光も鎌倉幕府の奉行人であった関係から、その養子となり鎌倉で元服した。
北条氏が滅亡すると上洛し、[[夢窓疎石]]の仲介により[[足利尊氏]]に仕官し、室町幕府の[[評定衆]]から守護奉行を経て、[[天竜寺]]の造営奉行を務めた。さらに[[僧録|禅律方]]奉行人となり主に[[禅宗]]寺院関連の案件を担当した。[[中先代の乱]]以後に衰亡した諏訪宗家の再興にも助力し、諏訪上社の花会が信濃一円の武士の御頭奉仕で行われるようになった。

'''諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠'''（すわ えんちゅう/おさか えんちゅう）（[[永仁]]3年（[[1295年]]） - [[貞治]]3年（[[1364年]]））は、[[鎌倉幕府]]と[[室町幕府]]の奉行人。[[諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|京都諏訪氏]]の祖。『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』の著者。
== 人物 == [[信濃国]][[諏訪郡]]生まれ。諏訪盛忠の子。[[諏訪神党]]の『神氏系図』によれば[[諏訪大社]]上社の支族で、[[埴科郡]][[四宮荘]]船山郷の[[地頭]]小坂氏を継いだので'''小坂円忠'''とも称する。そのほか、[[越中国]]高木村、[[近江国]]三宅郷十二里村・赤野井村地頭職を所領とした。諏訪氏は[[北条氏]]の得宗[[被官]]で、叔父の諏訪時光も鎌倉幕府の奉行人であった関係から、その養子となり鎌倉で元服した。
北条氏が滅亡すると上洛し、[[夢窓疎石]]の仲介により[[足利尊氏]]に仕官し、室町幕府の[[評定衆]]から守護奉行を経て、[[天竜寺]]の造営奉行を務めた。さらに[[僧録|禅律方]]奉行人となり主に[[禅宗]]寺院関連の案件を担当した。[[中先代の乱]]以後に衰亡した諏訪宗家の再興にも助力し、諏訪上社の花会が信濃一円の武士の御頭奉仕で行われるようになった。
[[延文]]元年（[[1356年]]）、尊氏の奥書を持つ『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』を完成させ、当時の公家社会をはじめとする各方面に諏訪信仰を普及させたほか、尊氏が発願した一国一寺の[[安国寺利生塔]]創建にあたり、[[安国寺 (茅野市)|信濃安国寺]]を諏訪上社の近くに建て、夢窓疎石を開山者とした。
また円忠は諏訪神党[[祢津氏]]が相伝した[[鷹匠]]の秘術の[[故実]]を受け継ぎ（『神氏系図』）、子孫の京都諏訪氏の惣領に代々伝授され、[[文明 (日本)|文明]]5年（1473年）には[[飛鳥井家|飛鳥井氏]]と諏訪忠郷が鷹に関する[[和歌]]に註釈をつけて[[将軍]][[足利義政]]の上覧に応えている（『鷹百首註』）。忠郷の子諏訪貞道も『鷹聞書少々』という鷹狩の故実書を編纂している。
円忠は和歌や連歌の道にも優れ、『[[新千載和歌集]]』、『[[新後拾遺和歌集]]』、『[[菟玖波集]]』に歌や句が選ばれた<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600]「神氏系図 前田古写本之写」</ref>。
== 関連項目 == * [[諏訪氏]]

== 参考文献 == *『長野県歴史人物大事典』 [[郷土出版社]]、1989年 *『信州の人物余聞』[[ほおずき書籍]]、2010年

円忠は和歌や連歌の道にも優れ、『[[新千載和歌集]]』、『[[新後拾遺和歌集]]』、『[[菟玖波集]]』に歌や句が選ばれた<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600]「神氏系図 前田古写本之写」</ref>。

'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|en|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|en|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|en|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|en|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|en|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Kyoto Suwa clan|en|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwadaimyōjin-e-kotoba|en|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|en|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|en|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|en|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|en|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|en|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|en|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|en|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|en|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|en|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|en|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|en|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|en|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwadaimyōjin-e-kotoba|en|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankokuji temples|en|安国寺利生塔}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|en|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|en|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|en|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|en|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|en|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|en|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|en|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|en|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|en|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|en|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|en|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|en|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|en|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|en|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|en|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|en|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|en|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Kyoto Suwa clan|en|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwadaimyōjin-e-kotoba|en|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|en|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|en|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|en|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|en|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|en|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|en|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|en|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|en|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|en|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|en|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|en|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|en|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwadaimyōjin-e-kotoba|en|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankokuji temples|en|安国寺利生塔}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|en|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|en|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|en|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|en|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|en|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|en|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|en|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|en|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|en|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|en|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|en|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|en|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== Related topics == * {{ill|Suwa clan|en|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|en|四宮荘}}

== References == * ''Nagano Prefecture Historical Person Dictionary''. Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1989. * ''Echoes of Shinshu People''. Hōzuki Books, 2010.

This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|en|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|en|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|en|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|en|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|en|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|en|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|en|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|en|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Kyoto Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwadaimyōjin-e-kotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwadaimyōjin-e-kotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankokuji temples|ja|安国寺利生塔}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Kyoto Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwadaimyōjin-e-kotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwadaimyōjin-e-kotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankokuji temples|ja|安国寺利生塔}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Kyoto Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏}}. '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Kyoto Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwadaimyōjin-e-kotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankokuji temples|ja|安国寺利生塔}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

== References == * ''Nagano Prefecture Historical Person Dictionary''. Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1989. * ''Echoes of Shinshu People''. Hōzuki Books, 2010.

His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.

Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.

'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.

'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.

Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.

Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qqqqqqqqqq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== Related topics == * {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}} * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}}
== Notes ==

== References == * ''Nagano Prefecture Historical Person Dictionary''. Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1989. * ''Echoes of Shinshu People''. Hōzuki Books, 2010.

Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|越中国}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "越中国" to "Etchū Province"
revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Bump-01-18]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295年}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364年}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473年}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== Related topics == * {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}} * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}}
== Notes ==

== References == * ''Nagano Prefecture Historical Person Dictionary''. Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1989. * ''Echoes of Shinshu People''. Hōzuki Books, 2010.

Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473rd year }}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "5年" to "5th year "
revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295th year }}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364th year }})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473rd year }}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]] on (CatAlot)
revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295th year }}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364th year }})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|1=Ankoku-ji|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473rd year }}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== Related topics == * {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}} * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘}}
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nagano Prefecture Historical Person Dictionary''. Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1989. * ''Echoes of Shinshu People''. Hōzuki Books, 2010.

After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|1=Ankoku-ji|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473rd year }}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|1=Ankoku-ji|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples|12=draft}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=draft}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=draft}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=draft}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=draft}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473rd year|12=draft}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家|12=draft}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=draft}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=draft}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== Related topics == * {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=draft}}

revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295th year|12=draft}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364th year|12=draft}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|12=draft}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|12=draft}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|12=draft}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞|12=draft}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|12=draft}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=draft}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭|12=draft}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=draft}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡|12=draft}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=draft}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏|12=draft}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石|12=draft}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|12=draft}}. He rose through the ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|12=draft}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=draft}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=draft}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=draft}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文|12=draft}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年|12=draft}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞|12=draft}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|1=Ankoku-ji|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples|12=draft}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=draft}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=draft}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=draft}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=draft}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473rd year|12=draft}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家|12=draft}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=draft}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=draft}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== Related topics == * {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=draft}}

23 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:諏訪円忠 up to rev 102637868
revision.text:
'''諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠'''（すわ えんちゅう/おさか えんちゅう）（[[永仁]]3年（[[1295年]]） - [[貞治]]3年（[[1364年]]））は、[[鎌倉幕府]]と[[室町幕府]]の奉行人。[[諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|京都諏訪氏]]の祖。『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』の著者。
== 人物 == [[信濃国]][[諏訪郡]]生まれ。諏訪盛忠の子。[[諏訪神党]]の『神氏系図』によれば[[諏訪大社]]上社の支族で、[[埴科郡]][[四宮荘]]船山郷の[[地頭]]小坂氏を継いだので'''小坂円忠'''とも称する。そのほか、[[越中国]]高木村、[[近江国]]三宅郷十二里村・赤野井村地頭職を所領とした。諏訪氏は[[北条氏]]の得宗[[被官]]で、叔父の諏訪時光も鎌倉幕府の奉行人であった関係から、その養子となり鎌倉で元服した。
北条氏が滅亡すると上洛し、[[夢窓疎石]]の仲介により[[足利尊氏]]に仕官し、室町幕府の[[評定衆]]から守護奉行を経て、[[天竜寺]]の造営奉行を務めた。さらに[[僧録|禅律方]]奉行人となり主に[[禅宗]]寺院関連の案件を担当した。[[中先代の乱]]以後に衰亡した諏訪宗家の再興にも助力し、諏訪上社の花会が信濃一円の武士の御頭奉仕で行われるようになった。
[[延文]]元年（[[1356年]]）、尊氏の奥書を持つ『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』を完成させ、当時の公家社会をはじめとする各方面に諏訪信仰を普及させたほか、尊氏が発願した一国一寺の[[安国寺利生塔]]創建にあたり、[[安国寺 (茅野市)|信濃安国寺]]を諏訪上社の近くに建て、夢窓疎石を開山者とした。
また円忠は諏訪神党[[祢津氏]]が相伝した[[鷹匠]]の秘術の[[故実]]を受け継ぎ（『神氏系図』）、子孫の京都諏訪氏の惣領に代々伝授され、[[文明 (日本)|文明]]5年（[[1473年]]）には[[飛鳥井家|飛鳥井氏]]と諏訪忠郷が鷹に関する[[和歌]]に註釈をつけて[[将軍]][[足利義政]]の上覧に応えている（『鷹百首註』）。忠郷の子諏訪貞道も『鷹聞書少々』という鷹狩の故実書を編纂している。
円忠は和歌や連歌の道にも優れ、『[[新千載和歌集]]』、『[[新後拾遺和歌集]]』、『[[菟玖波集]]』に歌や句が選ばれた<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600]「神氏系図 前田古写本之写」</ref>。
== 関連項目 == * [[諏訪氏]] * [[四宮荘]]
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *『長野県歴史人物大事典』 [[郷土出版社]]、1989年 *『信州の人物余聞』[[ほおずき書籍]]、2010年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すわ えんちゆう}} [[Category:諏訪氏|えんちゆう]] [[Category:信濃国の人物]] [[Category:鎌倉時代の人物]] [[Category:室町時代の人物]] [[Category:1295年生]] [[Category:1364年没]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[諏訪円忠]] to [[Suwa Enchū]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠'''（すわ えんちゅう/おさか えんちゅう）（[[永仁]]3年（[[1295年]]） - [[貞治]]3年（[[1364年]]））は、[[鎌倉幕府]]と[[室町幕府]]の奉行人。[[諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|京都諏訪氏]]の祖。『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』の著者。
== 人物 == [[信濃国]][[諏訪郡]]生まれ。諏訪盛忠の子。[[諏訪神党]]の『神氏系図』によれば[[諏訪大社]]上社の支族で、[[埴科郡]][[四宮荘]]船山郷の[[地頭]]小坂氏を継いだので'''小坂円忠'''とも称する。そのほか、[[越中国]]高木村、[[近江国]]三宅郷十二里村・赤野井村地頭職を所領とした。諏訪氏は[[北条氏]]の得宗[[被官]]で、叔父の諏訪時光も鎌倉幕府の奉行人であった関係から、その養子となり鎌倉で元服した。
北条氏が滅亡すると上洛し、[[夢窓疎石]]の仲介により[[足利尊氏]]に仕官し、室町幕府の[[評定衆]]から守護奉行を経て、[[天竜寺]]の造営奉行を務めた。さらに[[僧録|禅律方]]奉行人となり主に[[禅宗]]寺院関連の案件を担当した。[[中先代の乱]]以後に衰亡した諏訪宗家の再興にも助力し、諏訪上社の花会が信濃一円の武士の御頭奉仕で行われるようになった。
[[延文]]元年（[[1356年]]）、尊氏の奥書を持つ『[[諏方大明神画詞]]』を完成させ、当時の公家社会をはじめとする各方面に諏訪信仰を普及させたほか、尊氏が発願した一国一寺の[[安国寺利生塔]]創建にあたり、[[安国寺 (茅野市)|信濃安国寺]]を諏訪上社の近くに建て、夢窓疎石を開山者とした。
また円忠は諏訪神党[[祢津氏]]が相伝した[[鷹匠]]の秘術の[[故実]]を受け継ぎ（『神氏系図』）、子孫の京都諏訪氏の惣領に代々伝授され、[[文明 (日本)|文明]]5年（[[1473年]]）には[[飛鳥井家|飛鳥井氏]]と諏訪忠郷が鷹に関する[[和歌]]に註釈をつけて[[将軍]][[足利義政]]の上覧に応えている（『鷹百首註』）。忠郷の子諏訪貞道も『鷹聞書少々』という鷹狩の故実書を編纂している。
円忠は和歌や連歌の道にも優れ、『[[新千載和歌集]]』、『[[新後拾遺和歌集]]』、『[[菟玖波集]]』に歌や句が選ばれた<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600]「神氏系図 前田古写本之写」</ref>。
== 関連項目 == * [[諏訪氏]] * [[四宮荘]]
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *『長野県歴史人物大事典』 [[郷土出版社]]、1989年 *『信州の人物余聞』[[ほおずき書籍]]、2010年
