revision.text:
'''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|ja|永仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Einin|qq=}} 3 ({{ill|1295|ja|1295th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1295|qq=}}) – {{ill|Jōji|ja|貞治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōji|qq=}} 3 ({{ill|1364|ja|1364th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1364|qq=}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura shogunate|qq=}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|qq=}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province|qq=}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|qq=}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|jitō|ja|地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/jitō|qq=}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanishina District|qq=}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|qq=}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|qq=}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|ja|北条氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōjō clan|qq=}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|ja|夢窓疎石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musō Soseki|qq=}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga Takauji|qq=}}. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|qq=}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|qq=}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|qq=}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|qq=}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enbun|qq=}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1356|qq=}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏方大明神画詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|qq=}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|ja|安国寺利生塔|1=Ankoku-ji|Ankoku-ji=Ankoku-ji|lt=Ankokuji temples|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoku-ji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoku-ji|qq=}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|qq=}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|qq=}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|qq=}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|qq=}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Bunmei|ja|文明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmei|qq=}} 5 ({{ill|1473|ja|1473rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1473|qq=}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai clan|ja|飛鳥井家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asukai clan|qq=}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|qq=}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|qq=}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shin Chokusenshū|ja|新千載和歌集|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Chokusenshū|qq=}}, {{ill|Shin Goshūishū|ja|新後拾遺和歌集|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Goshūishū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|ja|菟玖波集|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukubashū|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== See Also == * {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}}
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nagano Prefecture Historical Person Dictionary''. Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1989. * ''Echoes of Shinshu People''. Hōzuki Books, 2010.
==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|qid=Q1131473}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|qid=Q2975376}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|qid=Q11631818}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q20040902]] Wikidata name: Suwa Enchū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ([[:en:Einin|Einin]] 3 ([[:en:1295|1295]]) – [[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 3 ([[:en:1364|1364]])) was an official of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]].
== Life ==
Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of [[:en:Jitō|jitō]] for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in [[:en:Hanishina District, Nagano|Hanishina District]], leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|qq=|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], entered the service of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|qq=|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] 1 ([[:en:1356|1356]]), Enchū completed the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]], which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of [[:en:Ankoku-ji|Ankokuji temples]], Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|qq=|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei]] 5 ([[:en:1473|1473]]), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the [[:en:Asukai family|Asukai clan]] on annotated falconry waka poetry for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== See Also == * [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]] * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}}

This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei]] 5 ([[:en:1473|1473]]), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the [[:en:Asukai family|Asukai clan]] on annotated falconry waka poetry for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|qq=|" to "|"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|WD=Q1131473}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20040902]] Wikidata name: Suwa Enchū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ([[:en:Einin|Einin]] 3 ([[:en:1295|1295]]) – [[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 3 ([[:en:1364|1364]])) was an official of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]].
== Life ==
Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of [[:en:Jitō|jitō]] for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in [[:en:Hanishina District, Nagano|Hanishina District]], leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], entered the service of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] 1 ([[:en:1356|1356]]), Enchū completed the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]], which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of [[:en:Ankoku-ji|Ankokuji temples]], Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei]] 5 ([[:en:1473|1473]]), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the [[:en:Asukai family|Asukai clan]] on annotated falconry waka poetry for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of [[:en:Ankoku-ji|Ankokuji temples]], Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei]] 5 ([[:en:1473|1473]]), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the [[:en:Asukai family|Asukai clan]] on annotated falconry waka poetry for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

<!--Suwa Enchū--><!--Suwa Enchū--><!--Suwa Enchū--><!--Suwa Enchū--> [[Category:People of Shinano Province]] [[Category:People of Muromachi-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q20040902}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|WD=Q1131473}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20040902]] Wikidata name: Suwa Enchū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ([[:en:Einin|Einin]] 3 ([[:en:1295|1295]]) – [[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 3 ([[:en:1364|1364]])) was an official of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]].
== Life ==
Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of [[:en:Jitō|jitō]] for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in [[:en:Hanishina District, Nagano|Hanishina District]], leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], entered the service of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] 1 ([[:en:1356|1356]]), Enchū completed the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]], which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of [[:en:Ankoku-ji|Ankokuji temples]], Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei]] 5 ([[:en:1473|1473]]), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the [[:en:Asukai family|Asukai clan]] on annotated falconry waka poetry for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

<!--Suwa Enchū--><!--Suwa Enchū--><!--Suwa Enchū--><!--Suwa Enchū--> [[Category:People of Shinano Province]] [[Category:People of Muromachi-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q20040902}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|WD=Q1131473}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20040902]] Wikidata name: Suwa Enchū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ([[:en:Einin|Einin]] 3 ([[:en:1295|1295]]) – [[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 3 ([[:en:1364|1364]])) was an official of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]].
== Life ==
Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of [[:en:Jitō|jitō]] for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in [[:en:Hanishina District, Nagano|Hanishina District]], leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], entered the service of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] 1 ([[:en:1356|1356]]), Enchū completed the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]], which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of [[:en:Ankoku-ji|Ankokuji temples]], Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei]] 5 ([[:en:1473|1473]]), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the [[:en:Asukai family|Asukai clan]] on annotated falconry waka poetry for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|WD=Q1131473}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20040902]] Wikidata name: Suwa Enchū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ([[:en:Einin|Einin]] 3 ([[:en:1295|1295]]) – [[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 3 ([[:en:1364|1364]])) was an official of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]].
== Life ==
Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of [[:en:Jitō|jitō]] for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in [[:en:Hanishina District, Nagano|Hanishina District]], leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], entered the service of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] 1 ([[:en:1356|1356]]), Enchū completed the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]], which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of [[:en:Ankoku-ji|Ankokuji temples]], Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei]] 5 ([[:en:1473|1473]]), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the [[:en:Asukai family|Asukai clan]] on annotated falconry waka poetry for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|WD=Q1131473}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20040902]] Wikidata name: Suwa Enchū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ([[:en:Einin|Einin]] 3 ([[:en:1295|1295]]) – [[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 3 ([[:en:1364|1364]])) was an official of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]].
== Life ==
Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of [[:en:Jitō|jitō]] for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in [[:en:Hanishina District, Nagano|Hanishina District]], leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], entered the service of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] 1 ([[:en:1356|1356]]), Enchū completed the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]], which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of [[:en:Ankoku-ji|Ankokuji temples]], Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei]] 5 ([[:en:1473|1473]]), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the [[:en:Asukai family|Asukai clan]] on annotated falconry waka poetry for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== See Also == * [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]] * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|WD=Q1131473}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20040902]] Wikidata name: Suwa Enchū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ([[:en:Einin|Einin]] 3 ([[:en:1295|1295]]) – [[:en:Jōji|Jōji]] 3 ([[:en:1364|1364]])) was an official of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]].
== Life ==
Born in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of [[:en:Jitō|jitō]] for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in [[:en:Hanishina District, Nagano|Hanishina District]], leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], entered the service of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In [[:en:Enbun|Enbun]] 1 ([[:en:1356|1356]]), Enchū completed the [[:en:Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba]], which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of [[:en:Ankoku-ji|Ankokuji temples]], Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei]] 5 ([[:en:1473|1473]]), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the [[:en:Asukai family|Asukai clan]] on annotated falconry waka poetry for [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the [[:en:Shinsenzai Wakashū|Shin Chokusenshū]], [[:en:Shingoshūi Wakashū|Shin Goshūishū]], and [[:en:Tsukubashū|Tsukubashū]].<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== See Also == * [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]] * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}}
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nagano Prefecture Historical Person Dictionary''. Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1989. * ''Echoes of Shinshu People''. Hōzuki Books, 2010.

Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. In {{ill|Enbun|en|Enbun|WD=Q1145071|lt=Enbun}} 1 ({{ill|1356|en|1356|WD=Q6130|lt=1356}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|en|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818|lt=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|en|Ankoku-ji|WD=Q10158536|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.

Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shinsenzai Wakashū|en|Shinsenzai Wakashū|WD=Q1143262|lt=Shin Chokusenshū}}, {{ill|Shingoshūi Wakashū|en|Shingoshūi Wakashū|WD=Q288358|lt=Shin Goshūishū}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|en|Tsukubashū|WD=Q3007417|lt=Tsukubashū}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:Wikidata has English label]]" to "[[Category:qq]]"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|WD=Q1131473}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20040902]] Wikidata name: Suwa Enchū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|en|Einin|WD=Q1327307|lt=Einin}} 3 ({{ill|1295|en|1295|WD=Q5560|lt=1295}}) – {{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|WD=Q1146153|lt=Jōji}} 3 ({{ill|1364|en|1364|WD=Q6185|lt=1364}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|en|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818|lt=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=jitō}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District, Nagano|en|Hanishina District, Nagano|WD=Q726661|lt=Hanishina District}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|en|Enbun|WD=Q1145071|lt=Enbun}} 1 ({{ill|1356|en|1356|WD=Q6130|lt=1356}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|en|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818|lt=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|en|Ankoku-ji|WD=Q10158536|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei}} 5 ({{ill|1473|en|1473|WD=Q6594|lt=1473}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai family|en|Asukai family|WD=Q11665942|lt=Asukai clan}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|WD=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shinsenzai Wakashū|en|Shinsenzai Wakashū|WD=Q1143262|lt=Shin Chokusenshū}}, {{ill|Shingoshūi Wakashū|en|Shingoshūi Wakashū|WD=Q288358|lt=Shin Goshūishū}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|en|Tsukubashū|WD=Q3007417|lt=Tsukubashū}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== See Also == * {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan}} * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}}
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nagano Prefecture Historical Person Dictionary''. Kyōdo Shuppansha, 1989. * ''Echoes of Shinshu People''. Hōzuki Books, 2010.
==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei}} 5 ({{ill|1473|en|1473|WD=Q6594|lt=1473}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai family|en|Asukai family|WD=Q11665942|lt=Asukai clan}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|WD=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shinsenzai Wakashū|en|Shinsenzai Wakashū|WD=Q1143262|lt=Shin Chokusenshū}}, {{ill|Shingoshūi Wakashū|en|Shingoshūi Wakashū|WD=Q288358|lt=Shin Goshūishū}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|en|Tsukubashū|WD=Q3007417|lt=Tsukubashū}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|WD=Q1131473}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818}}}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|en|Einin|WD=Q1327307|lt=Einin}} 3 ({{ill|1295|en|1295|WD=Q5560|lt=1295}}) – {{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|WD=Q1146153|lt=Jōji}} 3 ({{ill|1364|en|1364|WD=Q6185|lt=1364}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|en|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818|lt=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=jitō}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District, Nagano|en|Hanishina District, Nagano|WD=Q726661|lt=Hanishina District}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|en|Enbun|WD=Q1145071|lt=Enbun}} 1 ({{ill|1356|en|1356|WD=Q6130|lt=1356}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|en|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818|lt=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|en|Ankoku-ji|WD=Q10158536|lt=Ankokuji temples}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Nezu clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei}} 5 ({{ill|1473|en|1473|WD=Q6594|lt=1473}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai family|en|Asukai family|WD=Q11665942|lt=Asukai clan}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|WD=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shinsenzai Wakashū|en|Shinsenzai Wakashū|WD=Q1143262|lt=Shin Chokusenshū}}, {{ill|Shingoshūi Wakashū|en|Shingoshūi Wakashū|WD=Q288358|lt=Shin Goshūishū}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|en|Tsukubashū|WD=Q3007417|lt=Tsukubashū}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
== See Also == * {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan}} * {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}}

== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=jitō|1=Jitō}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District, Nagano|en|Hanishina District, Nagano|WD=Q726661|lt=Hanishina District|1=Hanishina District, Nagano}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Hōjō clan}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|en|Enbun|WD=Q1145071|lt=Enbun|1=Enbun}} 1 ({{ill|1356|en|1356|WD=Q6130|lt=1356|1=1356}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|en|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818|lt=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|1=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|en|Ankoku-ji|WD=Q10158536|lt=Ankokuji temples|1=Ankoku-ji}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=Falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Q11536451|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei|1=Civilization}} 5 ({{ill|1473|en|1473|WD=Q6594|lt=1473|1=1473}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai family|en|Asukai family|WD=Q11665942|lt=Asukai clan|1=Asukai family}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|WD=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shinsenzai Wakashū|en|Shinsenzai Wakashū|WD=Q1143262|lt=Shin Chokusenshū|1=Shinsenzai Wakashū}}, {{ill|Shingoshūi Wakashū|en|Shingoshūi Wakashū|WD=Q288358|lt=Shin Goshūishū|1=Shingoshūi Wakashū}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|en|Tsukubashū|WD=Q3007417|lt=Tsukubashū|1=Tsukubashū}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諏訪円忠]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1295|death_date=1364|birth_place={{ill|Suwa district|WD=Q1131473|1=Suwa District, Nagano}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|family={{ill|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|1=Suwa clan}}|notable_works={{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818|1=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba}}}} '''Enchū Suwa/Kosaka Enchū''' (諏訪 円忠/小坂 円忠) ({{ill|Einin|en|Einin|WD=Q1327307|lt=Einin|1=Einin}} 3 ({{ill|1295|en|1295|WD=Q5560|lt=1295|1=1295}}) – {{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|WD=Q1146153|lt=Jōji|1=Jōji}} 3 ({{ill|1364|en|1364|WD=Q6185|lt=1364|1=1364}})) was an official of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} and {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}, and the founder of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏#京都諏訪氏|Suwa clan=Suwa clan|lt=Kyoto Suwa clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan|qq=}}. He is the author of the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|en|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818|lt=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|1=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba}}.
== Life ==
Born in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, Suwa District, Enchū was the son of Suwa Moritada. According to the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, he was a branch member of the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine family and inherited the position of {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=jitō|1=Jitō}} for {{ill|Shinomiya-shō|ja|四宮荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinomiya-shō|qq=}} Funayama village in {{ill|Hanishina District, Nagano|en|Hanishina District, Nagano|WD=Q726661|lt=Hanishina District|1=Hanishina District, Nagano}}, leading to his alternate name, '''Kosaka Enchū'''. His holdings also included lands in {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Takagi Village, and the villages of Miyake-gō Jūniri and Akanoi in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}. The Suwa clan served as {{ill|gokenin|ja|被官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|1=hikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626601}} under the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Hōjō clan}}, and Enchū’s uncle Suwa Tokimitsu was also an official of the Kamakura shogunate, fostering Enchū’s upbringing and service in Kamakura.
After the fall of the Hōjō clan, Enchū traveled to Kyoto and, through the mediation of {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, entered the service of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}. He rose through the Ranks from {{ill|Hyōjōshū|ja|評定衆|1=Hyōjōshū|2=es|3=Hyōjōshū|4=ja|5=評定衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōjōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11288036}} to protectorate administrator, eventually overseeing the construction of {{ill|Tenryū-ji|ja|天竜寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天龍寺}}. He later became the administrator of {{ill|Zen temple affairs|ja|禅律方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen temple affairs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧録|1=Sōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10889387}}, managing Zen-related issues. Enchū also contributed to the revival of the Suwa clan, whose main line had declined after the {{ill|Revolt of Nakasendai|ja|中先代の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Revolt of Nakasendai|1=Nakasendai Rebellion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10871756}}, and helped establish a system where warriors across Shinano participated in the annual Suwa Upper Shrine festival.
In {{ill|Enbun|en|Enbun|WD=Q1145071|lt=Enbun|1=Enbun}} 1 ({{ill|1356|en|1356|WD=Q6130|lt=1356|1=1356}}), Enchū completed the {{ill|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|en|Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|WD=Q11631818|lt=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba|1=Suwa Daimyōjin Ekotoba}}, which bore a postscript by Takauji. This work spread Suwa faith throughout various strata of society, including the court nobility. As part of Takauji’s initiative to establish a national network of {{ill|Ankoku-ji|en|Ankoku-ji|WD=Q10158536|lt=Ankokuji temples|1=Ankoku-ji}}, Enchū founded {{ill|Shinano Ankokuji|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ankokuji|1=Ankokoji (Chino City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20043040}} near the Suwa Upper Shrine with Musō Soseki as its founding abbot.
Enchū also inherited knowledge of {{ill|falconry|ja|鷹匠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/falconry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鷹狩|1=Falconry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q211011}} from the {{ill|Nezu clan|ja|祢津氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 根津氏|1=Q11536451|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11536451}}, a branch of the Suwa clan, as recorded in the {{ill|Kami Clan Genealogy|ja|神氏系図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami Clan Genealogy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This expertise was passed down among the heads of the Kyoto Suwa clan, and in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei|1=Civilization}} 5 ({{ill|1473|en|1473|WD=Q6594|lt=1473|1=1473}}), Suwa Tadakuni collaborated with the {{ill|Asukai family|en|Asukai family|WD=Q11665942|lt=Asukai clan|1=Asukai family}} on annotated falconry waka poetry for {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|WD=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, recorded in the {{ill|Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja|鷹百首註|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Hyakushu-chū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Tadakuni’s son, Suwa Sadamichi, compiled a treatise on falconry titled {{ill|Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja|鷹聞書少々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Kikigaki Shōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Enchū also excelled in waka and renga poetry, with works selected for the {{ill|Shinsenzai Wakashū|en|Shinsenzai Wakashū|WD=Q1143262|lt=Shin Chokusenshū|1=Shinsenzai Wakashū}}, {{ill|Shingoshūi Wakashū|en|Shingoshūi Wakashū|WD=Q288358|lt=Shin Goshūishū|1=Shingoshūi Wakashū}}, and {{ill|Tsukubashū|en|Tsukubashū|WD=Q3007417|lt=Tsukubashū|1=Tsukubashū}}.<ref>[https://www.ro-da.jp/shinshu-dcommons/museum_history/03OD0624101600] "Kami Clan Genealogy: Copy of Maeda Manuscript"</ref>
